sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: To build stable Android applications, it is important to understand what powers a coroutine internally. Beyond launch { }, coroutines are controlled by CoroutineContext, Job, and Dispatchers which define their lifecycle and execution behavior.

sections.heading: 1. CoroutineContext
sections.content: CoroutineContext is a set of elements that define how a coroutine behaves. It acts like a configuration container.

sections.heading: Main Elements of CoroutineContext
items: Job: Controls the lifecycle of the coroutine.
CoroutineDispatcher: Determines which thread the coroutine runs on.

sections.heading: Combining Context Elements
sections.content: You can combine multiple context elements using the + operator.

sections.heading: 2. The Job (Lifecycle Management)
sections.content: A Job represents a coroutine and allows you to monitor or cancel it. It moves through specific lifecycle states.

sections.heading: Job States
items: Completing: Waiting for child coroutines to finish.

sections.heading: Parent-Child Relationship
sections.content: When launching a coroutine inside another coroutine, the parent Job automatically manages the child. Cancelling the parent cancels all children.

sections.heading: Parent Cancels Child Example
sections.content: If the parent Job is cancelled, all its child coroutines are cancelled automatically.

sections.heading: 3. Dispatchers (Thread Managers)
sections.content: Dispatchers determine which thread pool the coroutine uses. Choosing the correct dispatcher is essential for performance.

sections.heading: Common Dispatchers
items: Dispatchers.Main: Runs on the main UI thread.
Dispatchers.IO: Optimized for network, database, and file I/O.
Dispatchers.Unconfined: Advanced usage; starts in current thread but resumes elsewhere.

sections.heading: 4. withContext (Safe Thread Switching)
sections.content: withContext is a suspending function used to switch threads safely and return a result. It automatically switches back to the original context after execution.

sections.heading: Switching Context Example
sections.content: Start on Main, switch to IO for heavy work, and return automatically to Main.

sections.heading: 5. CoroutineScope vs CoroutineContext
sections.content: CoroutineScope and CoroutineContext are related but different concepts.

sections.heading: Key Differences
items: CoroutineScope: Holds a CoroutineContext and provides the ability to launch coroutines.
CoroutineContext: The actual configuration (Job, Dispatcher, etc.) that defines coroutine behavior.

sections.heading: Summary
items: CoroutineContext is the configuration container for a coroutine.
Dispatchers decide which thread pool executes the coroutine.
withContext safely switches threads and returns results.
CoroutineScope is a launcher that contains a CoroutineContext.
